metadata.model_full_name: Estimating the Circulation and Climate of the Ocean
metadata.framework: Diego's Core Model Capability Levels (MCL) v1.0

summary.primary_strength: Physics & Conservation (247 evidence items) - strictly closed budgets with finite-volume discretization
summary.gaps_identified: None - all dimensions at State-of-Art level

key_evidence.passage: The equations are discretized on a C-grid using a finite volume technique which has excellent conservation properties.
key_evidence.paper_title: Application of a parallel Navier-Stokes model to ocean circulation

key_evidence.passage: The finite volume form ensures that discrete integral properties such as conservation of mass and heat are maintained exactly to machine precision.
key_evidence.paper_title: Application of a parallel Navier-Stokes model to ocean circulation

key_evidence.passage: Total energy is conserved if the pressure work term sums to zero over the whole domain. In our scheme this is guaranteed by careful placement of variables on the C-grid and construction of the discrete divergence and gradient operators which are adjoints of one another.
key_evidence.paper_title: Efficient ocean modeling using non-hydrostatic algorithms

key_evidence.passage: The proposed scheme is based on a stochastic formulation of the inverse problem, combining a priori information about the unknown sound-speed field with measured data via minimization of a cost function.
key_evidence.paper_title: One-step analysis of non-linear traveltime data in ocean acoustic tomography

key_evidence.passage: The solution p to the nonlinear inverse problem is taken to be the parameter vector that minimizes the cost function J(p)
key_evidence.paper_title: One-step analysis of non-linear traveltime data in ocean acoustic tomography

key_evidence.passage: The speed of the front, c, is expected to be given by the Benjamin formula. The numerical solution is in excellent agreement with this theoretical prediction.
key_evidence.paper_title: Efficient ocean modeling using non-hydrostatic algorithms

key_evidence.passage: We compare our results to the linear theory of Long for steady, two-dimensional, non-rotating, stratified flow over an obstacle. For small amplitude obstacles the numerical solution matches the analytical solution.
key_evidence.paper_title: Efficient ocean modeling using non-hydrostatic algorithms

key_evidence.passage: The classic method to assess the realism of ocean simulations is to compare the time mean state of the simulated ocean with gridded climatological data sets such as Levitus.

One of the best tests of an ocean model's ability to simulate large-scale transport on decadal time-scales is to examine its uptake and redistribution of passive tracers such as CFCs and bomb radiocarbon.

The inverse problem is to estimate the temperature field from the travel-time measurements. We use a standard linear stochastic inverse method, which provides both a best estimate and formal error estimates.
key_evidence.paper_title: Acoustic observations of heat content across the Mediterranean Sea

key_evidence.passage: This method also yields formal error estimates for the inverted quantities. Here the a priori information is the mean and the covariance of temperature anomalies derived from historical hydrographic data.
key_evidence.paper_title: Acoustic observations of heat content across the Mediterranean Sea

key_evidence.passage: The goal of this thesis is to create a technique to adaptively refine a grid based on error estimates to achieve maximum accuracy for a given number of grid points.
key_evidence.paper_title: Adaptive error estimation in linearized ocean general circulation models

key_evidence.passage: We will also explore anisotropic refinement, which involves refining in one direction while coarsening in another.
key_evidence.paper_title: Adaptive error estimation in linearized ocean general circulation models

key_evidence.passage: We leveraged Google Earth Engine (GEE) to automatically access Landsat and Sentinel images and their metadata.
key_evidence.paper_title: AutoTerm: an automated pipeline for glacier terminus extraction using machine learning

key_evidence.passage: The network is trained on an NVIDIA A100 GPU, with the Adam optimizer.
key_evidence.paper_title: AutoTerm: an automated pipeline for glacier terminus extraction using machine learning

key_evidence.passage: A promising direction for resolving these multimodal challenges lies in the adoption of the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) guiding principles.
key_evidence.paper_title: Multimodal Issues and Key Technologies in Ocean Observation Data

key_evidence.passage: We thank V. Balaji for his work on the Flexible Modeling System (FMS), which provides the software infrastructure for the GFDL coupled climate models.
key_evidence.paper_title: Tracer Conservation with an Explicit Free Surface Method

key_evidence.passage: A major goal of the ECCO Consortium is to provide the best-possible, dynamically consistent, global ocean and ice data syntheses over a decade and longer time scales.
key_evidence.paper_title: A Consortium For Ocean Circulation And Climate Estimation

key_evidence.passage: To facilitate the broad usage of the synthesis results, ECCO will establish an efficient data server system.
key_evidence.paper_title: A Consortium For Ocean Circulation And Climate Estimation

comparison_reference.description: ECCO achieves maximum score across all dimensions
